question_frequency.rationale: The Pair asked zero didactic questions. It went straight to writing the full solution and explaining it post-hoc, never engaging the user at any decision point (table ordering, why include subtractive pairs, greedy vs. recursive, etc.).

question_quality.rationale: No choices were framed at all — no propose_options call, no forks surfaced. The crux of the problem (why subtractive pairs belong in the table, why descending order matters) is explained but never offered as a decision.

scaffolding.rationale: There is no question sequence to scaffold. The full solution and its rationale are revealed in one shot, which is the maximum possible spoiler.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm and the explanation (table trace, complexity note) is clear and actionable, but there is no back-and-forth — the user's only turn is a congratulatory wrap-up. Not a pedagogical exchange.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code is correct and equivalent to the baseline: identical NUMERALS table, identical greedy loop, all 11 test cases pass. Functionally on par.

friction.rationale: Slightly tighter than baseline (31s vs 45s, one fewer write attempt since it didn't chase the ~/exercism path). No pedagogical payoff to weigh against, but no extra friction either.
